Seemingly the logic of `_port_acquisition_delay` is not correct, your code logic is:

```
wait up to 10s to acquire the file lock
if acquiring lock succeeds, then release the lock immediately, if acquiring failed (timeout), then do nothing
```

But I expect the following logic:
```
wait until acquiring the lock successfully.
after acquiring the lock, holds the lock for 10s, and then release the lock.
```
Only this way, we can ensure we allocate 10s time for each task to run ray worker startup routine exclusively.

The only change here is to use a nested `atomic_directory` with `if short_venv.is_finalized; continue` being the new collision detection mechanism that was previously performed by an eager `os.symlink` in a `try` / `except`. To effect this change, instead of the structure `PEX_ROOT/venvs/short/<short hash> -> <real venv>` the structure becomes `PEX_ROOT/venvs/s/<short hash>/venv -> <real venv>`. Notably, 'short/' is replaced with 's/venv/'. SO we only gain 1 character for the short links.

Apparently the underlying windows API calls are working in UTF-16 and treating the code units as individual characters, when it ideally should return full code points, hence this workaround.

3) I agree with you that `description` is not a suitable place to show the info. So, I decide to remove them.

  - It can be built with `make docker-build-light`.
  - For me, the analysis looks as follows:
    ```
    # Build the light image without ffmpeg (without the changes on the PR)
    make docker-build-light
    docker tag localstack/localstack-light localstack/localstack-light-wo-ffmpeg
    # Get size of build with ffmpeg in bytes
    docker image inspect localstack/localstack-light-wo-ffmpeg | jq .[0].Size
    > 1864664703
    # Add the ffmpeg to the apt install / apply changes in the PR
    make docker-build-light
    # Get size of build with ffmpeg in bytes
    docker image inspect localstack/localstack-light | jq .[0].Size
    > 1993386742
    ```
  - So the addition of ffmpeg results in an increase of the uncompressed image size of 128722039 bytes or 122,76 MB.
  - (My results are a bit lower than yours because I added the ffmpeg install call to the apt install call in the base stage, where the cache is removed afterwards. Your `apt` call doesn't remove the apt cache.)
- The most interesting metric is the compressed image size (the one shown on DockerHub / the amount of data that needs to be downloaded).
  - I don't know of a way to reliably determine the compressed image size without pushing it to a registry.

While the 123 MB (uncompressed) seem like not too much, it's still an increase of 7% in image size just for this change. Since we have this use-case a lot, we try to tackle these problems by downloading the necessary software on demand (and cache it in the `var_libs` folder). In order to do that, we would need to have a statically linked ffmpeg build (assuming there are no issues with the license).

I'll add some comments to the code ;). (And do some renaming of variables) Basically, `functions` will map vars to functions, right? And since we walk the tolopological tree in order, I substitute every variable in connections by its corresponding function if it is already present in `function`. Due to the order, this is guaranteed to work. So e.g. if I have
```python
model_dict = {z: y, y: x}
```
then the order will be `[x, y, z]`. Then, since `x` is not a key in connectivity mapping, it gets ignored. For `y`, we add `{y: Function('y')(x)}` to `functions`. For `z`, we add `{z: Function('z')(Function('y')(x))}`, which upon printing looks a lot more readable:
```python
{y: y(x), z: z(y(x))}
```

d’après l'expert:

Il existe deux barèmes applicables en secteur foyer :
- l’APL 1 (cas général), qui concerne la plupart des logements-foyers, en particulier les résidences sociales neuves.
Les logements foyers éligibles à l'APL 1 foyer sont tous les logements foyers conventionnés neufs ou après travaux à compter du 1er avril 1979.
Les résidences sociales éligibles à l'APL 1 foyer sont :
• toute résidence sociale, conventionnée à ce titre pour la première fois, neuve ou après travaux (à compter du 1er janvier 1995),
• tout foyer jeunes travailleurs ou travailleurs migrants déjà éligible à l'APL 1 foyer faisant l'objet d'un nouveau conventionnement (à compter du 1er janvier 1995).

 

- l’APL 2 concerne les résidences sociales existantes conventionnées sans travaux depuis le 1er janvier 2005, et les foyers de jeunes travailleurs conventionnés sans travaux depuis le 1er octobre 1990.

Et dans la liste des tests j'ai remarqué que: 
- L'APL1 concerne les logements conventionnés.
- L'APL2 concerne les logements non conventionnés.

The recommendation as stated by RFC8624 does not provide an explicit reason as to why it makes that recommendation, but a qualified guess would be to avoid issues with systems running out of entropy or not having quality entropy.

I still think it's worthwhile to have an option to compile without deterministic ECDSA, though, for targets which have qualified entropy sources on-board. The idea of this PR is to detect a client disconnect even before the application starts to write the response, so the application may check at some strategic points if the client is still interested in the answer.

In a `select` loop, a socket that has been closed by the other side makes itself known by being readable (it is included in the readable list of the result of `select`), but if we read something, we get an empty answer. Therefore, we keep the channel `readable` (at least if there are not too many requests already queued from the channel) and if `handle_read` is then called and the read yields an empty response, this means the client has disconnected.

When we changed MIDI parsing to start creating time signatures or measures, people who were indexing into `myPart[0]` and expecting to find voices or notes were unexpectedly getting something else. For instance, if there were a clef and a key and a meter, the notes would probably start at index 3. And so if musicxml 5.0 starts implying 4/4 by default... you get the idea how this could break. But like I said, just something for future unless Myke feels strongly.

I went down a rabbit hole trying to get better GPIO performance by writing a c++ class using libgpiod.  Bottom line is even at the speeds I could get out of that library, the required pulse_time for the ws0010 is shorter than the time libgpiod takes to move the pin from low to high to low.  So effectively, the default pulse_time for the ws0010 (and winstar_weh) can be set to 0.  This significantly improves the update time for that display.  However, if you are trying to horizontally scroll anything, the upper limit appears to be around 30 FPS.  Above that, animations show tearing.  

I haven't done as much testing of the hd44780 displays but they appear to be much slower devices.  Setting pulse_time to zero does cause stability problems so we are stuck using at least some delay.  For now, I'll just leave the default to 1e-6 but may want to change from using sleep to a timing loop in future versions.  Also, 5 FPS was the sweet spot for refreshing it.

For a **user** account we don't need to check if the tenant is in `az account list`, as the `refreshToken` in `accessTokens.json` is the same for all tenants. **user : refresh token** is a 1:1 mapping. The refresh token can be used to retrieve a token in ANY tenant, even if the user is not a member of that tenant. The token doesn't has any permission in that tenant. 

A **service principal** is a binding between an **app** and a **tenant**, in other words, a projection of an **app** in a **tenant**. 

In `accessTokens.json`, the entry for a service principal account is:

```
{
    "servicePrincipalId": "6827...",
    "servicePrincipalTenant": "5482...",
    "accessToken": "43b2..."
}
```

`servicePrincipalId` is usually the `appId` returned by `az ad sp create-for-rbac`.

`accessToken` is actually the credential(password or certificate) of the service principal or app. If the credential is at app level, it is the same across tenants; if the credential is at service principal level, it is different across tenants. So **service principal : access token(credential)** is a 1:+ mapping. 

Even if for app credential, **service principal : access token(credential)** is a 1:1 mapping, we can't tell whether the credential is valid in other tenants until the AAD server returns error. I prefer not to do the bet. So to retrieve the credential, we need to use both `servicePrincipalId` and `servicePrincipalTenant`, which is searched at 

https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/fbfe69172600936798387e2b5c9c1c9887bb38f4/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/_profile.py#L940-L941

Therefore, for a Service Principal to work across tenants, we need to run `az login` twice for each tenant.

Yeah, at the moment this is very much for testing/experimentation. I don't know what form it will take in the long term, i.e. by 1.0. I think that will be informed in part by your iterator work, and perhaps vice versa. My current thoughts are as follows:

1) Allowing globbing, separate from selecting shards for use in the distributed case, is a useful feature. I'd like that to exist in the main library. No opinion on where. At the moment only `MultiprocessDatasetReader` allows this and I'm not using that because it doesn't scale linearly, to avoid flakiness, to avoid serialization overhead etc.

2) Sharding is very standard in distributed systems. To the extent that we want to support large datasets and multiple GPUs it seems natural for it to live in the main library.

3) Modulo `num_workers` is fine for small non-sharded datasets that don't do lots of generation in the reader. Happy to support that too. (I think this works in the lazy case as well?) Regardless, I'll need something like `ShardedDatasetReader` for the 1 billion word corpus.

I suspect, based on [Podman's documentation](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-info.1.html#example), that `podman info --format "{{ .host.security.rootless }}"` outputs `true` when in rootless mode and `false` otherwise. [Go Templates](https://pkg.go.dev/text/template#hdr-Actions) are quite powerful, so we can say: `docker info --format '{{ if .ClientInfo.Context }} {{ eq .ClientInfo.Context "rootless" }} {{ else }} {{ .host.security.rootless }} {{ end }}'`. This command outputs `true` when running in rootless mode and `false` otherwise on both Docker and Podman, although I have only tested it on Docker. Just wanted to bring this to attention again for discussion: I would prefer if we could not proceed with `pd.read_csv` if the return_code is non-zero.

Basically, there are at least 2 "kinds" of errors that can happen here, either the user enters a malformed shell cmd or the shell cmd is valid but the data itself is problematic (whether it is empty, or missing a comma, etc.) for `pd.read_csv` to parse.
Example of 1: `jn.io.read_commandline("catt data.csv")`
Example of 2: `jn.io.read_commandline("touch data.csv && cat data.csv")`

I am of the opinion that these 2 types of errors should not be categorized together. In particular, the former will (currently) lead to a pandas EmptyDataError, which is not actually the crux of the problem. I would very much prefer to catch the non-zero code, and raise a `JanitorError` containing the shell stderr.

Well the overloads simply specify which signatures are valid. For example, if the version is given as a string, assuming the string has all the information we need, we don't have to (and we shouldn't) pass any of the major, minor, patch, etc to the function. In our case, the type hint without overload would look like this: `(version_string_or_major: Union[str, SupportInt], minor: SupportsInt = None, ...)` which is ugly and is totally useless to the user of the function.

Now with overload it would look like this:
```python
from typing import overload

@overload
def __init__(self, version_string: str) -> Version:  # for Version("1.2.3", ...)
    ...

@overload
def __init__(self, major: SupportsInt, minor: SupportsInt, patch: SupportsInt) -> Version:  # for Version(1, 2, 3)
    ...

# We should do something about the kwargs like `prerelease` and `build`.
# Probably best to throw them in the implementation function signature.

# Do not put @overload here, because this is the actual implementation.
def __init__(self, *args, prerelease: String = None, build: String = None, **kwargs) -> Version:  # for Version(
    # the real implementation
```
And now we can call the function in each way:
```python
Version("1.2.4")  # correct
Version(1, 2, 4)  # correct
Version("1.2.4", 3, 2)  # type error (we should probably also raise a NotImplementedError at runtime)
```

Now you could chuck that code in there, but I still have doubts about how mypy will handle the `prerelease` and `build` kwargs in relation to the three main parts of the version. This would require further testing and experimentation. For now, I think it lays the foundation, and we can add more overloads as we find out what we can do correctly with this method.

> The read functions work based on the SSL/TLS records. The data are received in records (with a maximum record size of 16kB). Only when a record has been completely received, can it be processed (decryption and check of integrity). Therefore, data that was not retrieved at the last read call can still be buffered inside the SSL layer and will be retrieved on the next read call. If num is higher than the number of bytes buffered then the read functions will return with the bytes buffered. If no more bytes are in the buffer, the read functions will trigger the processing of the next record. Only when the record has been received and processed completely will the read functions return reporting success. At most the contents of one record will be returned.

```suggestion
    AsCoapMessage(aMessage).SetCode(MapEnum(aCode));
```

Suggest using `MapEnum(aCode)` instead of `static_cast<Coap::Code>(aCode)`.

`MapEnum` is itself defined in `as_core_type.hpp` and it converts the related `enum` types to each other. 
- It is simpler and safer to use in the sense that it only works between `enum` types that we explicitly relate using `DefineMapEnum()` (e.g., in this csae`otCoapCode` and `ot::Coap::Code` which are related in `coap_message.hpp`). 
- If we try to `MapEnum` between unrelated types, we get a compile error, while `static_cast` allows any `enum` type to be cast to any other type.

> I think users would expect that artifact_bucket_name: {{ stack_group_config.template_bucket_name }} would work as well

The problem here is that template_bucket_name (and many other items that might be in stack_group_config) might be resolvers. Especially with the recent set of merges to master, there are a lot more resolvable properties and "inheriting" a resolver is a lot more likely. Remember, the order of resolution within Sceptre is:

1. Gather raw stack group config variables from higher-level stack group config.yaml files. Since the yaml resolver constructs the resolvers, these variables might not be strings but instead might be raw resolvers.
2. (**"Render time"**) Use those stack group config and user variables to _render_ the config file using Jinja into a string. Thus `{{ stack_group_config.template_bucket_name }}` would cast the resolver to string rather than resolve it.
3. Parse the string into yaml, which will instantiate resolvers on the Stack.
4. Use that yaml to create the Stack instance, which assigns those resolvers to resolvable properties
5. (**"Resolve time"**) Then, upon accessing those resolvable properties, those resolvers are resolved into values.

Because of this, you cannot access resolved values at render time (step 2 above). This is the whole reason why I made the `!stack_attr` resolver, because we need to access other stack attributes (which are set at step 4 and resolved at step 4). Simply accessing the pre-resolved values at render time doesn't work. 

> if it doesn't then can you please provide some explanation as to why it would not work?

I hope the above works. However, I was thinking that one thing that hasn't been documented in Sceptre's docs is the "Stack construction lifecycle", similar to what I just wrote above. I'll add a docs page that explains this, since I find it's frequently confused in the conversations I've had with you and other maintainers lately.

> does the template handler doc need updating? https://github.com/Sceptre/sceptre-sam-handler#arguments section.

Yeah, it will. However, I didn't want to update it until this change has been released as a version. Otherwise, I'd be documenting functionality that doesn't exist yet. The extension repos make more sense if they "chase" Sceptre versions rather than attempt to preempt them.

I mean.... *of course* Coursera got "adverserial". I'm not sure how that's even an argument. They are obviously interested in making sure that you have to go and visit their website to see any of their content, because that's how they make their money. 

Additionally, installing recaptcha to protect your login page is very far from an act of war. I've been on the other side of this argument, and I can tell you that the only thing on the whole goddamn internet that can actually protect you against dedicated attackers with botnets is recaptcha. And you can be sure that Coursera has at least a few of those. All the other captcha solutions will only offer you protection for a day or a week – until whoever is on the other side figures it out. Recaptcha consistently works very well, and there are no other solutions that do, so it's no surprise that Coursera installed it. 

What is surprising is that this project has chosen not to address it, and to basically leave the whole project dysfunctional while hiding behind security arguments instead. That conditional at the end is to support using the decorator with 2 forms:
```
@instruction
def foo(...)
```
and this:
```
@instruction(can_take_denormalized_mod_imm=True)
def foo(...)
```
That is, allow using the decorator both with and without arguments.

In the first case, `body` will be bound to the function being decorated.  In the second case, however, `body` will be bound to the default value `None`.  If you were to write out the return type of `instruction`, it would be complicated, and would depend on exactly how it is called.

See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3888158/making-decorators-with-optional-arguments for some more explanation.

On net I think it is 👍🏻 , just set off alarms in my head.

For example, we are trying to run `cl run --- date; sleep 10`, in `sys.argv`, the parameters will be changed to `argv = ['run', 'date', ';', 'sleep', '10']`. If we don't leave the punctuation characters out and `shlex.quote` on every single subcommand, we will get the new `argv` as follows: ``` >>> argv = ['run', 'date', ';', 'sleep', '10'] >>> shell_excaped_argv = [shlex.quote(x) for x in argv] >>> shell_excaped_argv ['run', 'date', "';'", 'sleep', '10'] ``` Note that there is an additional pair of quote wrapped around semicolon. Then when it gets executed on real system, we get the following result: Basically, shell won't be able to recognize semicolon as a command separator if it wrapped with single quotes.

The specific method that triggered this change was `katcp.Sensor.parse_params`, which uses the sensor's underlying type to decode the parameters.  In this context, the word `formatted` in the variable name indicates that it should be "formatted" like it would be serialised on the wire (i.e., byte strings).
https://github.com/ska-sa/katcp-python/blob/df7f3a425c6387b9b3c125d5db7c37fdf748a472/katcp/core.py#L1568

When we use it from the inspecting client, we pass a `sensor_info` object that will have native strings instead of byte strings.  The change to native strings was part of the previous round of migration to Python 3.
https://github.com/ska-sa/katcp-python/blob/df7f3a425c6387b9b3c125d5db7c37fdf748a472/katcp/inspecting_client.py#L872-L874

Yes, we could fix the usages in inspecting client (and fake inspecting client) instead.  I'm not sure if there are other bad usages of `decode`, but I imagine they will show up easily enough.

Yeah, the assumption was that developers would want their changes forward-ported on updates (at least when working on the main branch), unless suppressed with `--no-update`. A merge conflict is a much more informative thing to give them than a rolled-back repository in that case, and doesn't touch their work. It felt like "hey, we know you want to update, but we're gonna remove the standard Git mechanism for dealing with merge conflicts".

It's all kinda moot with the `--ff-only` though. Now we assume that people never want to automatically forward-port changes instead. That solves the problem with work branches and is simple to get right, so it's probably better.

I agree that we should try to make it so that most users never have to touch the west/manifest repositories.

There're 2 approach reasonable to me.
1. Permanently disable cache and remove the overhead of key hashing. By this way, we can get best performance and minimal memory usage for the cache always miss scenarios.
2. Maintain cache size as user expected, and kick off an old one when reaching the memory limitation. Then we can expect cache may hit in future. Just like what cpu cache behaves.

> if the future inputs are consistently not in the cache then we need to constant remove one element every time which is expensive.

Removing an item from hash map has constant time complexity, which is much less than building key hashing. If you think it's expensive, then we should move to first approach(not hashing key at all).

Which is better? 

1. `http-proxy` for `ws://` and `https-proxy` for `wss://`.
2. Same as 1., but the default for `https-proxy` is the same as `http-proxy`.
3. Use `http-proxy` for both `ws://` and `wss://`.

I personally prefer 2. as it would kill two stones with one bird, there have been a few issues where people have needed to set `https-proxy` as well as `http-proxy`. As @bastimeyer mentioned, you have to know which connections streamlink will make - and sometimes we don’t even know which it will make, there could be redirects etc. 2. gives you a sensible default, but the ability to override it if needed, perhaps if your http proxy only supports https over https and http over http.

> Is there any harm in turning this into a call to `make_storage_roots`? Or maybe it could validate that the state roots have been generated, or maybe `make_storage_roots()` and `persist()` could be combined. Currently there's only one caller so the knowledge that one method must be called before the other isn't exactly distributed but it still feels like it belongs in `StorageDB`.

`VMState` always needs to know the output of `make_storage_roots()` so it can update the `AccountDB`. We could have persist return the updated storage roots, but it feels easy to miss the necessary step of capturing the storage roots coming out of `persist()`. Instead requiring that the caller explicitly handle the new storage roots before persisting seemed the cleanest. Especially since the storage roots are sometimes generated without persisting.

------

There is one corner case that I can see where the new code changes the behavior:
If we happen to have a prefix with a length strictly larger than 64 bit and its first 64 bits happen to match the mesh-local prefix and rest of bits happen to match the locator IID pattern, when calling `GetContext` we can get a different `Conext` matching `aAddress` than the mesh-local prefix one (since `GetContext()` tries to find the longest match length). So with existing code we won't treat the address as mesh-local (though it very much looks like it) but with new code (in this PR) we would. I don't know if this situation is realistic/valid and/or we need to handle.

If `readGlobalFile` does anything other than throw `NoSuchFileException` or return a path that exists, it is doing crimes, but `_abs()` in general is allowed to be called on nonexistent files (by `exists()`). So being called on one shouldn't be a failing assert.

> Systems that run automatic backups in the background might try to make a backup copy of an SQLite database file while it is in the middle of a transaction. The backup copy then might contain some old and some new content, and thus be corrupt

> If two processes have open connections to the same database file and one process closes its connection, unlinks the file, then creates a new database file in its place with the same name and reopens the new file, then the two processes will be talking to different database files with the same name. (Note that this is only possible on Posix and Posix-like systems that permit a file to be unlinked while it is still open for reading and writing. Windows does not allow this to occur.) Since rollback journals and WAL files are based on the name of the database file, the two different database files will share the same rollback journal or WAL file. A rollback or recovery for one of the databases might use content from the other database, resulting in corruption. A similar problem occurs if a database file is renamed while it is opened and a new file is created with the old name.

ref: https://www.sqlite.org/howtocorrupt.html

Please, use the backup api https://sqlite.org/backup.html

> Are these the default values for those options?

Nope.

> If they're not the default, do we have a strong reason to use them instead of the defaults? Is this going to be clearly beneficial to the code-base and to us as developers?

Let's go through them one by one:

- `disallow_untyped_defs`: by default `mypy` simply accepts untyped functions and uses `Any` for the input and output annotations. If our ultimate goal is to declare `torchvision` typed, we should make sure that we don't miss some functions. This flag enforces that.

- `no_implicit_optional`: By default `mypy` allows this:

  ```python
  def foo(bar: int = None) -> int:
      pass
  ```
  With this option enabled, it has to be
  ```python
  def foo(bar: Optional[int] = None) -> int:
      pass
  ```
  Given that `None` is a valid input, we should also explicitly mention it in the annotation.

- `warn_unused_ignores`: Sometimes we use `# type: ignore` directives on stuff that is actually wrong in other libraries. For example pytorch/pytorch#65998 will make some ignore directives obsolete that are needed now. Without this flag, we would never know.

- `warn_return_any`: If a function does something with dynamic types, `mypy` usually falls back to treating the output as `Any`. This will warn us if something like this happened, but we specified a more concrete output type. 

- `warn_unreachable`: This is more a test functionality, as `mypy` will now warn us if some code is unreachable. For example, with this flag set, `mypy` will warn that the `if` branch is unreachable.

  ```python
  def foo(bar: str) -> str:
      if isinstance(bar, int):
          bar = str(bar)
      return bar
  ```

- `allow_redefinition`: See #4531. If we have this globally, we can of course remove it here.

Apart from `warn_return_any` and `warn_unreachable` I think these flags are clearly beneficial. For the other two, they were beneficial for me in the past, but I can others object to them.

Here I tried to emulate the original behavior: if the user calls `MCP2221()` without the `bus`, it will check if the first device is already opened/cached and return that, if not it will try to open a different one below.

Other possible behaviors are:
* return one of the cached MCP (if any), even if it's not the first
* if no bus is specified, only try to open the first device

Note that simply importing the module will automatically create an instance of the first device, so any subsequent `MCP2221` call (without the `bus`) will return that instance.  This might not happen if another process already opened the first device -- in that case the class will try to open a different one.

This will currently return `None` if `ray.init()` has not yet been called. We should either update the return type annotation to `Optional[logger.Logger]` and add a note to the docstring that this will return `None` in that case, or we should raise an error in this function if `global_node is None`. For the former, the caller then needs to handle this case (see comment below for `DatasetLogger.get_logger()`). For the latter, we could do a quick check and raise an error, so the caller can assume that they'll always get a logger:
```python
        """Internal method to initialize the logger and the extra file handler
        for writing to the Dataset log file. Not intended (nor should it be necessary)
        to call explicitly."""
        # With current implementation, we can only get session_dir
        # after ray.init() is called. A less hacky way could potentially fix this
        global_node = ray._private.worker._global_node
        if global_node is None:
            raise ValueError("DatasetLogger._initialize_logger() must be called after ray.init().")
        logger = logging.getLogger(f"{self.log_name}.logfile")
        # We need to set the log level again when explicitly
        # initializing a new logger (otherwise can have undesirable level).
        logger.setLevel(LOGGER_LEVEL.upper())

        # Add log handler which writes to a separate Datasets log file
        # at `DatasetLogger.DEFAULT_DATASET_LOG_PATH`
        session_dir = global_node.get_session_dir_path()
        datasets_log_path = os.path.join(
            session_dir,
            DatasetLogger.DEFAULT_DATASET_LOG_PATH,
        )
        # Add a FileHandler to write to the specific Ray Datasets log file,
        # using the standard default logger format used by the root logger
        file_log_handler = logging.FileHandler(datasets_log_path)
        file_log_formatter = logging.Formatter(fmt=LOGGER_FORMAT)
        file_log_handler.setFormatter(file_log_formatter)
        logger.addHandler(file_log_handler)
        return logger
```

import threading
import os
import asyncio, ssl
import aioftp
class FtpServer(threading.Thread):
	def __init__(self):
		threading.Thread.__init__(self)
		self.setDaemon(True)
	def run(self,*args,**kwargs):
		self.loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
		asyncio.set_event_loop(self.loop)
		ssl_server = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2)
		ssl_server.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
		ssl_server.check_hostname = False
		ssl_server.load_cert_chain(os.path.dirname(__file__) + os.sep + 'ssl.crt', os.path.dirname(__file__) + os.sep + 'ssl.key')
		self.server = aioftp.Server(
			aioftp.MemoryUserManager(None),
			socket_timeout=60,
			idle_timeout=60,
			data_ports=range(30000,30010),
			ssl=ssl_server,
		)
		self.loop.run_until_complete(self.server.start(None, 990))
		self.loop.run_forever()
		
	async def terminateserver(self):
		await self.server.close()
		self.loop.call_soon_threadsafe(self.loop.stop)

	def stop(self):
		asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe(self.terminateserver(), self.loop)

ftp = FtpServer()
ftp.start()
while True:
	print('[ s ]: Stop')
	print('[ e ]: Exit')
	try:
		cmd = input('Input: \r\n')
	except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
		cmd = 'e'
	if cmd == 's':
		ftp.close()
	elif cmd == 'e':
		break

As you define 404 status code as non-error in [swagger](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/c803720c6bcfcb0fcf4c97f3463ec33a18f9e55c/specification/web/resource-manager/Microsoft.Web/stable/2021-01-01/AppServicePlans.json#L140-L142) , the generated python SDK will not raise exception as [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/f89776994d2a0292e6b10b9920f8828d5554143a/sdk/appservice/azure-mgmt-web/azure/mgmt/web/v2021_01_01/operations/_app_service_plans_operations.py#L250-L253) and azure CLI cannot catch the exception for resourceNotFound error.

There are two ways to fix:
1. Change swagger definition, which need to remove 404 part in response as [storage](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/0c057560083bfdfd50202f0e79cfac8ec054d56a/specification/storage/resource-manager/Microsoft.Storage/stable/2021-04-01/storage.json#L383-L398) and [network](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/0c057560083bfdfd50202f0e79cfac8ec054d56a/specification/network/resource-manager/Microsoft.Network/stable/2021-02-01/applicationGateway.json#L126-L140).
2. If you don't want to change swagger, you need to catch it in your code. In this way, I would recommend  you check response status code with 404. Currently you will raise resource not error when not plan, which is not accurate enough.

With a custom stdin filepath given, Black will try to format it in place as if it was a file:

```console
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/oss/black$ echo "print('hello world')" | black -v --stdin-filename="hello.py" -
Using configuration from /home/ichard26/programming/oss/black/pyproject.toml.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ichard26/programming/oss/black/src/black/__init__.py", line 706, in reformat_one
    if changed is not Changed.CACHED and format_file_in_place(
  File "/home/ichard26/programming/oss/black/src/black/__init__.py", line 845, in format_file_in_place
    then = datetime.utcfromtimestamp(src.stat().st_mtime)
  File "/opt/python3.8.5/lib/python3.8/pathlib.py", line 1194, in stat
    return self._accessor.stat(self)
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'hello.py'
error: cannot format hello.py: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'hello.py'
Oh no! 💥 💔 💥
1 file failed to reformat.
```

This is because `Path("-").is_file` returns `False` because a file doesn't actually exist at that path ([See `pathlib.Path.is_file` docs](https://docs.python.org/3.8/library/pathlib.html#pathlib.Path.is_file)). Therefore the path isn't prefixed with the magic string that tells `reformat_one` to use `format_stdin_to_stdout` and not `format_file_in_place`. Also `force-exclude` wouldn't be checked, but Black trying to format a file that doesn't exist is a bigger problem without this suggestion :-)

This function signature came out of my desire to break up the `holes` from the `outer` LinearRing. I was finding myself constantly making mistakes when the expected input was `[exterior, hole1, hole2, ...]`. For a simple polygon with no holes, it felt awkward to have to write it like `polyfill_polygon([outer])`. 

This all led to making `outer` the first ordered parameter, and adding `holes` as an optional parameter. You can't have non-keyword parameters **after** keyword parameters, so `res` had to have some default value.

I think there are a few different signatures we could use here. I'd love feedback on what you all think would be best.

1. `polyfill_polygon(outer, holes=None, res=9, order='latlng')`
    - current option
2. `polyfill_polygon(outer, holes=None, res=0, order='latlng')`
    - is `res=0` a better default?
3. `polyfill_polygon(outer, holes=None, res=None, order='latlng')`
    - `res` would now be a **required** keyword parameter; we'd raise an error if `res=None`
4. `polyfill_polygon(outer, res, holes=None, order='latlng')`
    - Eh, maybe. is it weird splitting up the `outer` and `holes` parameters?
    - Might be the cleanest option if the most common use case is for Polygons without holes
5. `polyfill_polygon(res, outer, holes=None, order='latlng')`
    - I think this is kind of what you were suggesting @isaacbrodsky. This makes it easy to curry the function, so you could specify `res` just once.
    - It does feel a little unintitive to have `res` first; and it doesn't match our current API.

The problem is that getters construct new objects based on what they find in `self._properties`, and once that is returned to the user, changes to those subtype instances are not reflected on the original `self._properties` dict (the parent type will not see the changes).

We could directly assigning a subdict from `_properties` as `_properties` of the child instance, but that would be fragile, e.g. multiple instances of a subtype would share the same `_properties` dict (yikes!).

Or did I misunderstand the idea?

It's probably the most straightforward if we keep the state in SQL type objects and only convert to/from `_properties` dicts when needed. Users that want to directly manipulate the `_properties` dict could still do so, they would just need to re-construct the instance:
```py
foo_type._properties["bar"] = "new_bar"
foo_type = foo_type.from_api_repr(foo_type._properties)
```
Would that be acceptable?

After all that's messing with internals, and a user consciously doing that should know that the changes are not automatically reflected on the manipulated instance.

Somehow, running `make clean-all checks` the target for `.coverage` failed because of a Python file of an installed package, that has been registered by the `find` call, but did not exist anymore.

My theory:
Running make, it first evaluates the dependencies between the single targets, also calling `find` in this case. This generates a list with all Python files of the installed packages at each start of a run. Then the target `clean-all` removes all this packages. The packages are installed  again as a dependency of the `checks` target, but maybe some versions of dependent packages could have changed, which may result in different Python files as registered at start. This leads to errors, like
```
No rule to make target '.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/some-package/some-file.py', needed by '.coverage'.  Stop.
```

That is why I think, the folder `.venv` has to be excluded.

Get(PublishedPackage, PublishRequest, S3PublishRequest(publish_target=":s3-bucket", fieldset=S3Bucket()))

One idea that @datumbox and I discussed a while back is abolish the close correspondence of the dtype and the value range for images. Right now we assume the range `[0, 1]` for floating point images and `[0, torch.iinfo(dtype).max]` for integral images. A possible solution for this would be have a `value_range` meta data on the `features.Image` class. It can be set with the defaults from above so for a regular use case the user won't feel any difference. 

For example, let's say we have a uint8 image

```py
import torch
from torchvision.prototype import features

image = features.Image(torch.randint(0, 256, (3, 128, 128)))
```

By default `print(image.value_range)` would print `(0, 255)`. If we now convert the dtype like `image = image.to(torch.float32)`, printing the value range again would give `(0.0, 255.0)` rather than `(0.0, 1.0)` what happens through `ConvertImageDtype`.

This means, all transformations that implicitly rely on the value range like `Normalize` need to be adapted to use the explicit value instead, but we could get rid of `ConvertImageDtype` all together.

__new__ is when a new object is being created. We are using a metaclass, so this is being called when a class becomes visible to benchbuild.

If we use __init__ or __init_subclass__, we would get the completed class handed to us. Now, while __init__ works just fine (did in the past, there is nothing wrong with it), annotating it with correct type information is a nightmare.
You have no idea about type-information of your descendants in the metaclass, so the assumed type is Any. No type Any has NAME/DOMAIN/GROUP... class attributes.

This above is way cleaner. During class construction we do not have to make any assumption about the descendant class of this metaclass. Before construction, we simply check the attributes that are about to be added to the constructed class.
However, we need to do check the complete type hierachy of the descendant class for any required attributes, because they can be defined in a superclass.

 The only thing I don't like about this, is simply that we have to filter abstract/partial experiments properly (which is not a bad thing). Otherwise, they end up in the experiment registry and cause a mess when doing a run over all experiments (mostly a feature used during testing, but hey...).

On C5, we do the same as the paper and we use the layer just before pooling.

On C4, we deviate from the original paper that suggests using the "the **expansion** layer of the 13th bottleneck block". Good questions, let me start from where I found the issue...

When trying to sync up to the upstream v11.0.0 release, I was getting test failures for China and some Islamic countries (Turkey, Malaysia adn Qatar). While poking around these Islamic failures, I realised that the two Eid holiday "series" were not being shifted correctly: only the day in the series that actually fell on a Sunday was being shifted, and that shift then caused it to be a duplicate of the next day in the Series.

This then fell foul of the Workalendar support for ical export (introduced circa Aug 2020). This includes tests which assert, amongst other things, on the uniqueness number of `uid_lines`...which the duplicates now broke.

I realised at this point that I could not see any logic in either Workalendar or Calendra to deal with this - except that I had already seen the custom logic that handled Christmas day and Boxing day (recall issue #15 is why I was here in the first place).

Note: since we have your observance day logic as distinct from what upstream does  (which is why I choose to use Calendra over Workalendar), there are two different codebases with the same problem! I immediately realised that I had little prospect of a decent/viable fix for upstream, but your logic provides easy detection AND a basis for a somewhat tacky solution. The current implementation is a direct consequence of this train of thought.

(Once I implemented it for Malaysia, I realised the same fix was needed for the other Islamic countries, and shortly later for the various series holidays in China).

The shortcomings of the solution are:

1. No obvious backport upstream.
2. As you point out, the lack of a code structure corresponding to the problem in hand.  Conceptually at least, this is broken since it cannot handle the obvious possibility that the series itself may have a knock on effect on a later holiday.
3. No immediate backport to the Christmas/Boxing day series. This is a direct consequence of the conceptual problem from 2.
4. I especially don't like the need to tailor the copy-pasting of the observance for each year in case the shift point moves, and all the duplication of Holiday. (Part of that at least could be optimised but...).

I felt obliged to ignore 1, and the solution for 3 would have been intrusive. As for 2, I considered subclass of Holiday to wrap the series, but could not see a comfortable fit for the observed_date() API:

- A covariant return like `Union[date, List[date]]` seemed horrid
- A new observed_dates() API seemed worse.

So that's why I ended up with what you see.

Finally, with hindsight, it would definitely have been better as a separate commit ahead of the sync. But I didn't think about that at the time, and not having the fix meant that v11.0.0's tests would not pass. Moving it now would be tedious, but may be the right thing to do (not least because it would make filing a bug upstream easier). Your call...

[`pyarrow.Array.null_count`](https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/a32f454d86560ab357b419273bb08678e1632d30/python/pyarrow/array.pxi#L986) forwards the call to underlying c++ implementation of `arrow::Array` (the [`sp_array` attribute](https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/a32f454d86560ab357b419273bb08678e1632d30/python/pyarrow/array.pxi#L799) is a shared pointer to the CArray). `arrow::Array` seems to always being able to compute the number of nulls, going by [the docstring](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/cpp/api/array.html#_CPPv4NK5arrow5Array10null_countEv), so apparently there're no cases of `.null_count` returns a -1.

The protocol's [mentioning that "Arrow may return -1"](https://data-apis.org/dataframe-protocol/latest/API.html#:~:text=Note%3A%20Arrow%20uses%20%2D1%20to%20indicate%20%22unknown%22%2C%20but%20None%20seems%20cleaner.) seems to be referencing [these lines in arrow doc](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/cpp/api/array.html#:~:text=If%20known%2C%20the%20null%20count%20can%20be%20provided%20in%20the%20base%20Array%20constructor.%20If%20the%20null%20count%20is%20not%20known%2C%20pass%20%2D1%20to%20indicate%20that%20the%20null%20count%20is%20to%20be%20computed%20on%20the%20first%20call%20to%20null_count()) describing passing precomputed number of nulls to the Array's constructor, which doesn't correlate with explicit `.null_count()` request.

import time

num = 1000000

def f(d):
    return

def f_kwargs(**d):
    return

d_in = {str(i): 0 for i in range(100)}
start = time.time_ns()
for _ in range(num):
    f(d_in)
delta = time.time_ns() - start
print("delta / num={}".format(delta / num))

delta = time.time_ns() - start

**`UpdateChildAddresses` is called in 3 places:**

1. `HandleChildIdRequest` which can change Child state from `Invalid` to `ChildIdRequest` or 
2. `HandleChildUpdateRequest` which can change Child state from `Restoring` to `Valid`
3. `HandleChildUpdateResponse` which can change Child state from `ChildUpdateRequest` to `Valid`

One thing I noticed is that in both case 2 and 3, `UpdateChildAddresses` is called before `SetChildStateToValid`. So, I think it necessary to also consider `Restoring` state since it just going to be `Valid` immediately. 

For case 1 (`HandleChildIdRequest`),  `UpdateChildAddresses` seems to be called when the Child state is still invalid. In such case, no address will be processed by `MlrManager`, which I think is an issue.

What do you think if we remodel this around a "mixin"-type approach? This would mean that we could create the following classes that can then be combined (`apply` takes an array):

```
AuthenticatedPlugin # checks to see if users are authenticated (regardless of protected mode)
ProtectedPlugin # if protected mode is set, checks to see if users have been granted access
```

This means that our current classes would map to the following:

`apply=ProtectedPlugin()` -> `apply=ProtectedPlugin()`
`apply=AuthenticatedPlugin()` -> `apply=AuthenticatedPlugin()`
`apply=AuthenticatedProtectedPlugin()` -> `apply=[AuthenticatedPlugin(), ProtectedPlugin()]`

Really, this change would just involve removing `AuthenticatedProtectedPlugin()`, but I think it makes it easier to see what is going on and lets you compose more plugins in the future.

A "namespace package" is a very specific thing in Python (see https://peps.python.org/pep-0420/#namespace-packages-today) etc. And this isn't checking for any of that. I think it's assuming that the parent of any module with a `.` in it is a namespace package, but that need not be the case.

And anyway, I don't think you actually need to know about namespace packages at all. I think there's a simpler solution here, similar to what I outlined in the [discussion](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/discussions/17286) but even more straightforward.

Basically, instead of the new enum, we can track the distance of the parent (0=we found the symbol itself, 1=we found its parent, etc.)

Then we resolve ambiguities by taking the provider with the smallest distance. And only if there are multiple of those do we warn and skip the dep.

> Do we need __hash__ implemented too here?

`__hash__` will fail noisily if it is not implemented, so you're always safe to not have it unless you determine that some codepath needs it. Using a value as a `Param` (at the root of the graph, or as the input to a `Get`) requires both `__hash__` and `__eq__`. If something is not being used as a `Param`, then `__eq__` only comes into play to improve caching behavior, as described in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/10309#discussion_r452597829

This addresses the second race condition in the description. The problem here is that we call `CreateActorOnWorker ` is called as a callback of `Put`. This means that `DestroyActor` which calls `CancelOnWorker` can be called before `CreateActorOnWorker` is called (in my integration scenario). Since `CancelOnWorker` checks if the client is already connected or not (to GC), it is broken there. => https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/726c4cd6d44ca10850fc4490b8fd04d0e009390a/src/ray/gcs/gcs_server/gcs_actor_scheduler.cc#L137

There is a couple solution. 

1. We make the connection before calling `Put` => this solution.
2. Just relieve the invariant check within `OnCancelNode`, meaning instead of checking if connection is already made, we just add an if statement and erase.

The change is correct! `r''` strings have nothing to do with regular expressions.

```
$ python -W default
Python 3.8.3 (default, May 17 2020, 18:15:42) 
[GCC 10.1.0] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> r'\,'
'\\,'
>>> '\,'
<stdin>:1: DeprecationWarning: invalid escape sequence \,
'\\,'
```

The backslash is ambiguous; it *could* mean "give me the character represented by the escape sequence 'comma'", but there is no such escape sequence listed in https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#string-and-bytes-literals
> Unlike Standard C, all unrecognized escape sequences are left in the string unchanged, i.e., the backslash is left in the result.

So the failover mode when you add C-style escape sequences that don't exist, is to treat it as a string literal. But this is deprecated behavior, and future versions of python will make it a hard error.

Using `r''` string literals merely disables this ambiguity by ensuring the string is not parsed for C-style escape sequences, which is fine as you don't have any. The python interpreter parses the source code the same way.

When a user defines a class extending `Resource`, Pydantic will use the type annotations to build a schema of valid values for each field.

For example, if the user defines the resources:
```python
class MyCredentials(Resource):
    username: str
    password: str

class MyResource(Resource):
    creds: MyCredentials
```

Then the schema requires `creds` to be an instance `MyCredentials` when `MyResource` is constructed. However, we would like to enable a user to specify a runtime-configured resource, which is of the type `Partial[MyCredentials]`.

To do so, we want to redefine the user's type, behind the scenes, to:
```python
class MyResource(Resource):
    creds: MyCredentials | PartialResource[MyCredentials]
```

This is what this metaclass does - when the user defines a class extending `Resource`, it iterates over all the fields and remaps any `Resource`-annotated field with a union of `Resource` and `PartialResource`.

CXX=`"${R_HOME}/bin/R" CMD config CXX`

CPPFLAGS=`"${R_HOME}/bin/R" CMD config CPPFLAGS`

I think your proposal makes a lot of sense, and I have questions about the same cases as you have questions: (1) what to do for `refonfail=True` case 4/5 for "user", and (2) error vs warning behavior in some cases.

> While if the object is only defined in other "user" modules (except __main__), it may be fair to save it as a reference to one of them (cases 4 and 5, builtin), or alternatively to raise an error too. This last case is the single one I'm not sure of how it should behave.

I'd say, since it's `refonfail=True`... then this makes sense.  I'm wondering if there's a case where it fails in `mod` and is then saved by reference to `other.name` or similar... but the object is actually defined in `mod`, and `other.name` is just a reference to the original in `mod`.  The question is: is it possible for this to go wrong... where save-by-reference to `other.name` is the wrong thing to do.  I think we need to try to think up failure scenarios for this case.

> With refonfail=False, we can either save the module by reference, preferebly with a warning, or raise an error. As this is not the default, I think that raising an error is fair, suggesting to the user the use of filters to exclude the offending object from the dump.

I think I agree here.

> For "user" modules with default options, an unpickleable object will only raise an exception if it can't be found elsewhere (or only in __main__). This case could force the module to be saved by reference and raise a warning.

See my first comment.

> For refonfail=False, I think it should always raise an error.

I agree here.

Thanks for the thoughtful comments! 

I suspect that the behavior Eric describes as "simple and consistent" only seems so to us, because we are so used to thinking in these terms.  The behavior Eric describes as "confusing" is, I think, exactly what users both expect and want. 

For example, I think that to end users it would be pretty intuitive that depending on a `python_distribution` means building it and installing it into the environment of the dependee. 

This gets to the fact that we have - in practice - "inner" or "code" targets that own files (python_library, python_tests, files, resources), and then "artifact" targets (python_distribution, pex_binary) that don't own files but instead represent an artifact to be built. We don't model this distinction explicitly, but we probably should. Maybe we should even use different terms for them.  Depending on an "artifact target" can mean something different than depending on a "code target", and I think that meaning is fairly unambiguous: depending on an edge target means depending on whatever is built from it, and *not* depending transitively on its own deps. 

I think this is a more user-centric way of thinking about it than requiring the burden of a special-case field. 

> In your test, you can unzip the .whl and inspect that everything is there that you expect. 

That is not useful, unless you're testing Pants itself. A built pex_binary may be "data", in some sense, but a built python_distribution is not - it has to be installed into the environment.  Concretely, if you have a native extension you expect to be able to use it in your tests. That requires building and installing the dist. Making people jump through weird hoops here seems not great.

The complicating issue, no matter how we end up supporting this, is what to do with dep inference. If a symbol is provided by the built artifact, that we don't want to also infer a dep on the source of that symbol. And this will be the case even if we use some special-cased field.

I'm not motivated by NEP 29. Personally, I think the right approach to take is, "keep open as many user options as is easy to do so," which is a two-parameter optimization: ease of users to connect versions X and Y of two libraries and our ease of providing version X that works with Y. That means there's some sort of weighting between user hardship and developer hardship that shouldn't be taken too extreme in either direction. But with most of the tools we use dropping Python 2 support at the end of this year, our developer hardship would go through the roof if we tried to continue maintaining it. That's the reason we'll be giving up on Python 2 users, if there are any of them left. (I don't have good survey data for this year, but last year it was still significant.)

By putting the support window on a schedule, NEP 29 is very friendly toward downstream dependencies, since they can plan for loss of support. However, most of our users are not developers and likely wouldn't know where to look for that schedule if it even occurs to them that there might be one. I think as long as we're not more restrictive than NEP 29 (supporting fewer versions), then we won't be a burden on anyone who does want to follow it (specifically, developers of downstream dependencies). Surprising them with a lack of support would be a problem, but I don't see how it could be bad to surprise them with more support than they want.

Looking at the source and testing locally, I can confirm that both re and regex cache compiled patterns. Somehow I still observe a 8-9% speed up on that strings list literal file. Running this test script:
```python
import time
from functools import lru_cache

import re
import regex

_cached_regex_compile = lru_cache(regex.compile)

# This is *roughly* how many times regex.compile was called formatting strings-list.py
loops = 410 
pattern = r"\s*\t+\s*(\S)"

re.compile(pattern)
regex.compile(pattern)

t0 = time.perf_counter()
for i in range(1, loops + 1):
    re.compile(pattern)
elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
print(f"{loops} cached compiles with re: {elapsed * 1000:.1f}ms")

t0 = time.perf_counter()
for i in range(1, loops + 1):
    regex.compile(pattern)
elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
print(f"{loops} cached compiles with regex: {elapsed * 1000:.1f}ms")

t0 = time.perf_counter()
for i in range(1, loops + 1):
    _cached_regex_compile(pattern)
elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
print(f"{loops} cached compiles with regex + lru_cache: {elapsed * 1000:.1f}ms")
```
I get:
- 410 cached compiles with re: 0.4ms
- 410 cached compiles with regex: 1.6ms
- 410 cached compiles with regex + lru_cache: 0.1ms

So it seems like this is the source of the slight speedup, but oddly enough this only accounts for ~half of the 8-9% speedup I saw for fmt-strings-list. Maybe the fact this script only repeated compiles one regex is why the delta isn't 1:1. For real life cases this speedup is probably minimal so I wouldn't mind reverting this to be less confusing :)

So, in my previous response, I used neutral and factual statements, but the sense of all of this is that stupid Windows is stupid. Or maybe Python on Windows, or both. But I will say Windows, because Python developers have to deal with the same poor API, like all of us.

The situation we have here occur when we execute Certbot under administrative shell (as it is required for a lot of operations). Let's say we create a file through Python: the root cause is here, instead of having the owner being the actual user (like me, `Adrien Ferrand`), it is the `Administrators` group (that I belong to). First stupidy so.

Let's say now we call our `filesystem.chmod`, that creates the proper ACL list for user, and everyone. The method will get the current user, and construct the ACL list. In fact in this specific case, the byte for user is ignored. Indeed, as the owner is `Administrators`, it is superseeded by the all privileges always given to this group.

So far, so good.

But, for private keys, we use the octal `0o400`. So read for user, nothing for everyone. Here happens the second stupidity. Despite me being part of the `Administrators` group, who has all privileges, Windows fails to say to me that I have in fact all permissions. In the UI for instance, when you check the DACL, Windows says that it cannot show the permissions, you need to explicit invoke elevated privileges to see it.

And here happens the third stupidity: `os.remove` uses the same way to resolve privileges. It does not see the permissions effectively given to user through its membership to `Administrators` group, and so raise a `Permission denied`.

Of course, I could find a way to mitigate that in the tearDown of impacted test classes. First thing I tried was to invoke `filesystem.chmod(path, 0o777)`, to give back permissions to everyone and allow Windows to see the permissions I have on the file. Surprisingly, it does not work, and I still get a permission denied. More precisely, it works afterward, when the Python process finishes, and I am able to remove the file from the UI without any privilege permissions.

I could try to understand why, or find another way to do that, like reimplementing `os.remove` using some magic function in `pywin32` to make low API calls directly with elevated privileges.

But I do not want to do that. Because the real problem here, is that the owner of the file is not the one that is supposed to be. It should be the current user, not the `Administrators` group. Indeed, it raises two absurd situations:
* first, as a user from the `Administrators` group, I cannot manipulate the files for which I have effectively full access to, and provokes the situation we have here with `os.remove`
* second, if I live the `Administrators` group, I lose every access on files on which `0o400` has been applied: despite read permissions have been required for owner, he has none of them ...

So the proper way to solve that, is to ensure that upon file creation, the proper owner is used. This is done in a future PR, that implements the ownership manipulation functions in `filesystem`, and insert in particular a correction of this ownership in the `filesystem.open` and `filesystem.mkdir` functions.

As I said some times, the windows file permissions project is a complete set of actions with a lot of coupling necessary to work correctly (like here, file modes, ownership and creation file). I tried to split it to ease the integration into small PRs, but this is at the cost of breaking temporarily some coupling. Here it is the case.

I hope I answered exhaustively to the situation, and convinced you to just leave this temporary mitigation done in a test class as it is, until the full logic is merged.
